one_line_takeaway: A legal-plus-ML position paper that quantifies FL's privacy-energy trade-off (FL ~5x less energy-efficient than centralized to reach 50% accuracy on a BERT task) and argues the EU AI Act could drive FL adoption only if the community reprioritizes energy efficiency, data governance, and privacy-mechanism compliance.
key_finding: FL currently pays a steep, quantified efficiency penalty that the AI Act's forthcoming energy/sustainability requirements will make costly: to reach 50% accuracy (TTA50) on the BERT/20NG task, FL is ~5x less energy efficient than centralized training (computation + communication combined), takes 4.3x longer in wall-clock time-to-accuracy, and uses 3.19x more computation energy; communication is 26% of total energy draw and client idle time during validation is 31%. Layering DP privacy on small systems requires large noise z that further hurts accuracy and stability, producing an unresolved privacy-energy trade-off. Cryptographic privacy (SMPC/HEC) scales worse than DP by algorithmic cost, and HEC's block on server-side evaluation may make it hard to satisfy the Act's robustness/quality-monitoring duties. FL's inherent advantages (data lineage, access to siloed/representative data for bias mitigation, no data movement) position it well for compliance if these efficiency and data-governance gaps are closed.
core_contribution: A position/interdisciplinary (legal + ML) analysis arguing that the EU AI Act can be a catalyst for mainstream adoption of Federated Learning (FL) if the FL community reprioritizes its research. The paper's OWN contributions are three: (1) a requirement analysis mapping AI Act / GDPR provisions (data governance Art.10, security-in-processing GDPR Art.32, robustness Art.15, energy/sustainability Art.40/69) onto concrete FL technical challenges; (2) a quantitative case study fine-tuning a 110M-parameter BERT with FL on the 20 News Group dataset that surfaces a 'privacy-energy trade-off' (increasing (epsilon,delta)-DP noise multiplier z degrades accuracy and training stability while energy costs stay high) plus an energy cost model P = Pc + Pt (computation + communication) and a bit-wise communication-energy formula; and (3) a distilled list of future FL research priorities (data-quality techniques without direct data access, CO2-based optimization, expression of privacy under the Act, privacy-technique alignment, and a compliance/benchmarking framework). The catalogue of attacks (gradient inversion, membership inference, data poisoning) and privacy mechanisms (SMPC, HEC, DP) are drawn from cited work as motivation, not introduced here.
threat_model: No novel threat model is proposed and no attack is executed; this is a position paper. It references FL's known attack surface from cited work as motivation for why privacy mechanisms are needed under the AI Act / GDPR: model-update sharing during training exposes an attack vector enabling gradient-inversion and membership-inference attacks (Geiping et al. 2020, Huang et al. 2021), FL is vulnerable to client-side data-poisoning, and stored/transmitted data is exposed to man-in-the-middle and unauthorized access. The paper treats the FL service provider as the party held liable under the Act for robustness, quality, and privacy, and frames DP/SMPC/HEC as defenses whose regulatory adequacy (e.g., whether (epsilon,delta)-DP satisfies GDPR privacy) is unresolved.
limitations: Single narrow empirical setup: one model (BERT-110M), one dataset (20 News Group), one small client count (k=10), so the 5x/4.3x/3.19x figures are illustrative of one high-risk-analogue pipeline rather than a broad benchmark. Only (epsilon,delta)-DP is measured empirically; SMPC and HEC are assessed by asymptotic complexity, not runtime/energy measurement. Analysis is pinned to a Jan 23 2024 draft of the (then-unfinalized) AI Act, with key provisions (energy Code of Conduct, standardization under Art.40) not yet defined, so several 'requirements' are anticipated rather than settled. The core question — whether DP or any technical privacy mechanism legally satisfies GDPR/AI Act privacy — is explicitly left open. As a position paper it argues a normative direction (redirect FL research effort) rather than delivering a validated method or framework.

datasets_used: 20 News Group (email/newsgroup text classification), used for FL fine-tuning of BERT and framed as a high-risk job-application pre-screening analogue
quantitative_results: FL is ~5x less energy efficient than centralized training (computation + communication) to reach 50% accuracy / TTA50 on the BERT/20NG task
FL takes 4.3x longer wall-clock time-to-accuracy (TTA50) than centralized (Figure 2)
FL uses 3.19x more computation energy than centralized (Figure 2)
Communication accounts for 26% of FL total energy draw
Client idle time (during validation) consumes 31% of all power
(epsilon,delta)-DP sweep at k=10, delta=1/16,000: epsilon ranges from 69.8 (z=0.6) up to 1,396,262.3 (low z) — all far above the epsilon<=1 tight-privacy target
Full-model fine-tuning to TTA50 (footnote 2): 0.48 kWh computation and 196 kWh communication, i.e. 2100x more than PEFT, communicating 110M parameters over 1,057 rounds
Model: BERT with 110M parameters; parameter precision typically b=32 bits
EU AI Act max penalty: EUR 35M or 7% of worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher (Art. 71.1)
Cumulative GDPR penalties approaching EUR 4.5B over the five years to January 2024
CO2 certificate price under ETS-2 projected to rise ~6x, from EUR 45 (2024) to EUR 300, with the market mechanism effective 2027
FL requires low-bandwidth interconnects (<1 GB/s) vs centralized multi-GPU (>200 GB/s NVLink/Infiniband)
baselines_compared: Centralized training vs Federated (k=10) — the empirical energy/time-to-accuracy baseline
(epsilon,delta)-DP (Andrew et al. 2021) — the only privacy mechanism run empirically (noise-multiplier z sweep)
SMPC (Bonawitz et al. 2017) — compared analytically by algorithmic cost only (Table 1)
Homomorphic Encryption / HEC (Jin et al. 2023) — compared analytically by algorithmic cost only (Table 1)
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) vs full-model fine-tuning — energy comparison
